elements.sent-2411-001: Among all metrics based on p-norms, the Manhattan (p=1), euclidean (p=2) and Chebyshev distances (p=infinity) are the most widely used for their interpretability, simplicity and technical convenience.
elements.sent-2411-002: But these are not the only arguments for the ubiquity of these three p-norms.
elements.sent-2411-003: This article proves that there is a volume-surface correspondence property that is unique to them.
elements.sent-2411-004: More precisely, it is shown that sampling uniformly from the volume of an n-dimensional p-ball and projecting to its surface is equivalent to directly sampling uniformly from its surface if and only if p is 1, 2 or infinity.
elements.sent-2411-005: Sampling algorithms and their implementations in Python are also provided.
elements.sent-2411-007: In mathematics, for instance, for $p\geq 1$, the $p$-distance function $(\bfx, \bfy)\mapsto \norm{\bfx-\bfy _p$ is a metric for $\bbR^n$.}
elements.sent-2411-008: This metric, called the Minkowski distance, is the basis for more complex and deeply studied metric spaces in mathematics such as.
elements.sent-2411-009: In machine learning, the $p$-norms are used as regularization penalties to reduce overfitting ---mainly L1 and L2 penalties, and as loss functions in regression problems.
elements.sent-2411-010: In statistics, the $p$-norms can be used to define the most fundamental central tendency statistics as $m_p(X) \eqdef \inf_m E(\norm{X-m _p)$.}
elements.sent-2411-011: Notably, the mean occurs with $p=2$, the median with $p=1$, the midrange with $p=\oo$, and as some authors suggest, the mode with “$p=0$”, an abuse of notation that is arguably wrong because three different limits occur as $p\to 0^+$.

elements.sent-2411-013: Geometrically, the $p$-norms have a clear interpretation for $p\in\set{1,2,\oo}$.
elements.sent-2411-014: By considering all points in $\bbR^n$ at $p$-distance at most 1 and exactly 1 from a given central point, we obtain the $p$-ball and the $p$-sphere respectively, and these have simple geometrical shapes for $p\in\set{1,2,\oo}$, e.g. a rhomb, a circle and a square for $n=2$.
elements.sent-2411-015: For these values of $p$, the Minkowski distance receives special names.
elements.sent-2411-016: It is called Manhattan or taxicab distance for $p=1$, euclidean distance for $p=2$ and Chebyshev or maximum distance for $p=\oo$.
elements.sent-2411-017: For other values of $p$ the $p$-norms are less studied, perhaps because of unrealistic or unclear semantics and mere algebraic inconvenience.
elements.sent-2411-018: In this paper, we show that the values $p\in\set{1,2,\oo}$ are not merely more practical in terms of geometry or algebra, but they are also special in terms of spatial uniformity.
elements.sent-2411-019: We prove that these norms and only these norms have the property that the $n$-dimensional volumetrically uniform distribution on the $p$-ball induces the ($n-1$)-dimensional superficially uniform distribution on the $p$-sphere via a linear projection.
elements.sent-2411-020: The prefix ``squi-'' comes from the resemblance of a $p$-circle, or ``squircle'', between a square ($p=\oo$) and a circle ($p=2$).. .
elements.sent-2411-021: The parametrization of the circumference (visible in Figure~\ref{fig:2D ) is analogous to $(\cos(t),\sin(t))$ varying $t$ in $[0, 2\pi]$, except that $\pi$ and the functions sine and cosine become $\pi_p$, $\cos_p(t)$ and $\sin_p(t)$ (depicted in Figure~\ref{fig:p-perimeters ), with the following properties:.}}
elements.sent-2411-022: More precisely, from Section 3.1 of \cite{wood2020squigonometry-chaps3-7 , $\sin_p(t)$ and $\cos_p(t)$ are defined for $p<\oo$ as the unique solution to the coupled initial value problem\begin{equation}C'(t) = -S(t)^{p-1} ;\quad S'(t) = C(t)^{p-1} ;\quad C(0) = 1 ;\quad S(0) = 0, \tagEquation\label{eq:squigonometric-ode}\end{equation} which converge as $p\to\oo$, to $\sin_\oo(t) \eqdef \cos_\oo(t-2)$ and \begin{equation}\cos_\oo(t) \eqdef  \min(1, \max(-1, |t-4|-2)) if  t\in[0, 8] \cos_\oo(t \mod 8) otherwise. \end{equation}.}

elements.subsection-2411-001: If there is a p-length, why not a p-area?
elements.sent-2411-040: Notice that the sides of a rectangle with vertices $(0,0)$, $(a,0)$, $(0,b)$, $(a,b)$ are $a$ and $b$ regardless of the $p$-norm being used, hence the area is independent of $p$, at least for straight (non-tilted) rectangles.
elements.sent-2411-041: If we assume the following minimal assumptions for a notion of area,.
elements.sent-2411-042: For non-pathologic figures, the total area of the rectangles that touch the figure border (overestimating the total area) converges to zero as the resolution increases, hence also the error of the approximation converges to zero.
elements.sent-2411-043: Since this limiting procedure yields the same result independently of the norm $p$ used to measure the sides of the rectangles, the notion of area for figures in $\bbR^2$ is independent of $p$ and coincides with the classic euclidean measure of area.
elements.sent-2411-044: For length, however, when segmenting a curve into infinitesimal diagonal segments, the length of each diagonal depends on the spatial norm.
elements.sent-2411-045: While it could be argued that if the curve was segmented into horizontal and vertical segments, the length would be the same for all $p$-norms, this is known to be an invalid approximation for the length of a curve.
elements.sent-2411-046: For surfaces in 3D, the approximation consists of infinitesimal inclined planes, and the area of each plane depends on the $p$-norm used to measure its diagonal sides.
elements.sent-2411-047: The same dependency holds for lengths in 3D, while the volume in 3D is the same for all $p$-norms as it is approximated with straight cubes.
elements.sent-2411-048: More generally, for $\bbR^n$ endowed with the $p$-norm, the $n$-dimensional measure does not depend on $p$ but all $k$-dimensional measures with $1\leq k<n$ do.
elements.sent-2411-049: An important remark is that the lengths of the diagonals and the area of inclined planes must be computed without rotating them, because all $p$-norms are sensitive to rotations, except only for the euclidean norm.
elements.sent-2411-050: More precisely, as shown in Theorem 5.5 of~\cite{macarthur2023classifying , when $p\in[1,\oo)$ but $p\neq 2$, there are only 8 isometries (transformations that preserve size) in 2D that fix the origin (to exclude translations), namely, the identity, the reflections across the $y$-axis, $x$-axis, the line $y=x$, the line $y=-x$, and the rotations by $\pi/2$ , $\pi$, and $-\pi/2$.}
elements.sent-2411-051: So there are only 4 rotations for $p\neq 2$ (defining a rotation in 2D as an isometry that fixes the origin and only the origin), while for $p=2$ there are infinitely many.
elements.sent-2411-052: For this reason, at least from an abstract point of view, $p=2$ provides the richest structure and is the most special of all $p$-norms.
elements.sent-2411-054: As in the 2D case, we will focus on the subset in which all coordinates are non-negative.
elements.sent-2411-055: Let us call this set the unsigned $n$-dimensional $p$-sphere, denoted $|\Sph^n_p|$, with absolute value in the notation to denote non-negativity.
elements.sent-2411-057: To parametrize the unsigned $p$-sphere, let us use the following recurrence formula that simplifies the generalization to $n$ dimensions.

elements.sent-2411-059: The objective is to compare the hyper-volume $V(t)$ enclosed by the light blue and green shades, with the hyper-area\footnote{This means area in 3D, volume in 4D, $(n-1)$-dim.-volumes in $n$D, and length in 2D.}
elements.sent-2411-060: For this comparison, we shall introduce two constants.
elements.sent-2411-061: Let $R$ be the hyper-area of the orange shade, which is contained in the hyper-plane $z=0$, and let $P_q$ be the hyper-length\footnote{This means length in 3D, area in 4D, volume in 5D, etc., and set cardinality in 2D.}
elements.sent-2411-062: The hyper-area $R$ does not depend on $Q$, because it is the mass of an $n-1$-dimensional object that is embedded in $n-1$ dimensions without rotation (by discarding the last dimension).
elements.sent-2411-063: However, the hyper-length $P_q$ does depend on $q$, analogously to the discussion in the previous section.
elements.sent-2411-064: In particular, for $n=3$, $P_q$ coincides with $\Lpq(\pi_p/2)$ and $R$ with $A(\pi_p/2)$.
elements.sent-2411-065: To integrate the volume, we shall consider the dark blue and dark green hyper-disks of differential heights.
elements.sent-2411-066: The top and bottom hyper-surfaces of these hyper-disks, are scaled versions of the same orange shape.
elements.sent-2411-067: Notice that when this shape is scaled on every dimension by a factor $c$, the hyper-area changes by a factor $c^{n-1}$, because $p$-norms are linear to scalar multiplication and the shape is $(n-1)$-dimensional.
elements.sent-2411-068: Hence, the hyper-area of the blue hyper-disk contained in the plane $z=\cos_p(t)$ for some $t$ is $\sin_p(t)^{n-1 R$, and the hyper-area of the dark green hyper-disk at height $z=\cos_p(t)$, is $R \cos_p(t) \lr{\frac{\sin_p(t_0) {\cos_p(t_0) ^{n-1}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-069: This yields \begin{equation}V(t_0) = \int_0^{t_0} R \sin_p(t)^{n-1}\, d\cos_p(t) + \int_{t_0}^{\pi_p/2} R \left(\frac{\sin_p(t_0) \cos_p(t)}{z(t_0)}\right)^{n-1}\, d\cos_p(t) = R \int_0^{t_0} \sin_p(t)^{n-1}\, d\cos_p(t) + R \frac{\sin_p(t_0)^{n-1}}{z(t_0)^{n-1}} \left.\frac{\cos_p(t)^{n}}{n}\right|_{t_0}^{\pi_p/2} = R \int_0^{t_0} \sin_p(t)^{n-1}\, d\cos_p(t) + \frac{R}{n} \sin_p(t_0)^{n-1} z(t_0)\end{equation} from which we can conclude that $V(t)$ is differentiable and its derivative is \begin{equation}\frac{d\,V(t)}{dt} = R \sin_p(t)^{n-1} \frac{d\,\cos_p(t)}{dt} - \frac{R}{n} \;\frac{d}{dt} \left( \sin_p(t)^{n-1} \cos_p(t) \right) = R \sin_p(t)^{n+p-2} + \frac{R (n-1)}{n} \sin_p(t)^{n-2} \cos_p(t)^p - \frac{R}{n} \sin_p(t)^{n+p-2} = \frac{R}{n} \sin_p(t)^{n-2} (n \sin_p(t)^{p} + (n-1) \cos_p(t)^p - \sin_p(t)^{p}) = \frac{R}{n} \sin_p(t)^{n-2} (n-1).\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-007: \begin{equation}V(t_0) = \int_0^{t_0} R \sin_p(t)^{n-1}\, d\cos_p(t) + \int_{t_0}^{\pi_p/2} R \left(\frac{\sin_p(t_0) \cos_p(t)}{z(t_0)}\right)^{n-1}\, d\cos_p(t) = R \int_0^{t_0} \sin_p(t)^{n-1}\, d\cos_p(t) + R \frac{\sin_p(t_0)^{n-1}}{z(t_0)^{n-1}} \left.\frac{\cos_p(t)^{n}}{n}\right|_{t_0}^{\pi_p/2} = R \int_0^{t_0} \sin_p(t)^{n-1}\, d\cos_p(t) + \frac{R}{n} \sin_p(t_0)^{n-1} z(t_0)\end{equation}

elements.sent-2411-083: In this section we develop and discuss computational methods to sample uniformly from the unitary $p$-ball in $n$ dimensions, both volumetrically inside the ball and superficially on the frontier.
elements.sent-2411-084: An implementation of these algorithms in Python can be found in the appendix.

elements.sent-2411-097: The gaussian distribution has the distinctive property that if $X$ and $Y$ are independent and gaussian, then the joint density function $f_{X,Y (x,y)$ of the vector $(X, Y)$ is isotropic (i.e. radially symmetric), as it can be written as a function of the euclidean norm $f_{X,Y (x,y) = f(x) f(y) = g(\norm{(x,y) _2)$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-098: Moreover, the joint of independent distributions is isotropic if and only if it is a scaled version of the gaussian.
elements.sent-2411-099: The argument is the following\footnote{A video visualizing and explaining the proof can be found in~\cite{youtube3blue1brown .. .}}
elements.sent-2411-100: For $y=0$ we have $f(x) f(0) = g(|x|)$, so letting $a=\sqrt{|x|}$, $b=\sqrt{|y|}$, and $h(r) \eqdef g(\sqrt{r ) / f(0)^2$, the radial symmetry equation becomes \begin{equation}h(a^2) h(b^2) = \frac{g(a)}{f(0)^2} \frac{g(b)}{f(0)^2} = \frac{f(\pm a)}{f(0)} \frac{f(\pm b)}{f(0)} = \frac{g(\norm{(\pm a,\pm b)}_2)}{f(0)^2} = h(a^2 + b^2).\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-012: \begin{equation}h(a^2) h(b^2) = \frac{g(a)}{f(0)^2} \frac{g(b)}{f(0)^2} = \frac{f(\pm a)}{f(0)} \frac{f(\pm b)}{f(0)} = \frac{g(\norm{(\pm a,\pm b)}_2)}{f(0)^2} = h(a^2 + b^2).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-101: This functional equation is known as the Exponential Cauchy's Functional Equation~\cite{kannappan2009functional-2-2 , and the only non-degenerate\footnote{Pathological solutions exist~\cite{book:28165471 by means of Hamel bases.}}}
elements.sent-2411-102: See~\cite{chen2016monsters for a quick and clear explanation for the “monstrous” solutions to Cauchy's additive functional equation, which are analogous to the exponential via a logarithmic transformation.}
elements.sent-2411-103: Therefore, $f$ must be given by $f(x) = f(0)\, e^{-c |x|^2}$ for some constant $c$.
elements.sent-2411-104: For $f$ to be a density, we must have $c>0$ and $f(0)=c/\sqrt{2\pi}$, where the mysterious appearance of the constant $\pi$ is due precisely to the radial symmetry of the gaussian, a beautiful connection and celebrated fact for which at least 11 different proofs have been found~\cite{conrad2016gaussian .}
elements.sent-2411-105: With a similar argument, it can be shown that a distribution is radially symmetric in the $p$-norm for $p<\oo$ if and only if it is given by $f(x) = f(0)\, e^{-c |x|^p}$ for some constant $c>0$.
elements.sent-2411-106: For $p=\oo$, the argument is slightly different.

elements.sent-2411-149: This contradiction is a consequence of the Borel-Kolmogorov paradox.
elements.sent-2411-150: In the classic paradox, we consider a uniform distribution over a sphere and wish to express the conditional distribution restricted to a meridian line (half circumference) going from pole to pole.
elements.sent-2411-151: By means of spherical coordinates, with a high resolution grid of latitudes and longitudes, one finds that if the domain is restricted to any thin lune bounded by consecutive longitudes, then the distribution of area is uniform over the lune while the distribution of latitudes is more concentrated around the equator, where the lune is thicker.
elements.sent-2411-152: As the resolution increases, the lune approaches a meridian path from pole to pole, and the distribution of latitudes converges to $f_\theta(\theta) \propto \sin \theta$, which is maximal at the equator ($\theta=\pi/2$) and minimal at the poles ($\theta \in\set{0,\pi}$).
elements.sent-2411-153: But the distribution of longitudes for a fixed latitude does converge to a uniform distribution in $[0,2\pi]$, which in turn implies a uniform distribution in $[0,\pi]$ when the domain is restricted to one half of the equatorial circumference.
elements.sent-2411-154: This difference between the distribution of latitudes on a fixed meridian line and that of longitudes over a half of the equatorial line contradicts conventional wisdom, because the two curves are identical and the spherical shape and the uniform distribution on its surface are invariant under rotations.
elements.sent-2411-155: There is no reason to believe that they should be different.
elements.sent-2411-156: The solution to the paradox is that the conditional distribution given the a fixed event depends on the limiting procedure used to approach the event when the event has probability zero (a null event).
elements.sent-2411-157: This implies that conditional distributions on null events should always have a subscript indicating the limiting procedure, or a context from which it is obvious.
elements.sent-2411-158: According to~\cite{jaynes2003probability470 , “...it is difficult to get people to see that the term ‘great circle’ is ambiguous until we specify what limiting operation is to produce it.}
elements.sent-2411-159: The intuitive symmetry argument presupposes unconsciously the equatorial limit; yet one eating slices of an orange might presuppose the other.” He argued that in absence of information about a limiting procedure, it does not make sense to speak about conditional distributions given null events.
elements.sent-2411-160: But recently, some researchers have proposed that in absence of a reference limiting procedure the Hausdorff measure should be understood~\cite{bungert2020lion .}
elements.sent-2411-161: After all, the fact that there is no unique universal way of defining distribution on null events in a way that is invariant to the limiting procedure does not disprove that there is a default canonical limiting procedure.
elements.sent-2411-162: In this paper, we share and promote this view that the canonical $k$-dimensional measure in $n$-dimensions is the $k$-dimensional Hausdorff measure, defined and explained in full detail in~\cite{edgar2008measureHausdorff .}
elements.sent-2411-163: Indeed, we have been referring to lengths of curves of surfaces in $\bbR^n$ without questioning whether the curves have been approached as very thin worms or very thin rosaries or cones.
elements.sent-2411-164: In the case of Algorithms~\ref{alg:surface-coord and \ref{alg:surface-normal , Equation~\eqref{eq:disintegration is indeed correct and the density $f_{\bar X}$ is constant, but this does not imply that the distribution of $\bar X$ is uniform over the $p$-sphere in the canonical sense.}}}
elements.sent-2411-165: This is why the distribution generated by Algorithm~\ref{alg:surface-coord is uniform on the $p$-sphere (in the canonical sense), while that of Algorithm~\ref{alg:surface-normal is not.}}
elements.sent-2411-166: The latter ensures volumetric uniformity between two $p$-spheres of infinitesimally close $p$-radii, which is not the same as superficial uniformity unless $p\in\set{1,2,\oo}$, as proven in Proposition~\ref{prop:nD .}
elements.sent-2411-167: This paper has shown that the $p$-norms $p\in\set{1,2,\oo}$ in $n$-dimensional space ($n\geq 2$) are the only to enjoy of a volume-surface correspondence, meaning that when the volumetric uniform distribution inside the unitary $p$-ball ($X\in\Ball_p^n$) is projected to its surface ($X/\norm{X _p \in \Sph_p^n$) one obtains a uniform distribution on the surface.}
elements.sent-2411-168: Four algorithms for sampling uniformly from the volume and surface of the $p$-ball have been derived and they are implemented in Python in the Appendix.

elements.sent-2411-170: The last two rely on the $p$-normal distributions, the only distributions to have a joint-isotropic property, meaning that the joint density of $n$ i.i.d.
elements.sent-2411-171: Counterintuitively, the volume algorithms are equivalent in distribution, but the surface algorithms are not, unless $p=\set{1,2,\oo}$.
